speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Ione Adair took place at her home in Moscow on September 3rd, 1976. Bernadine Adair Cornelison also took part in the talk. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: She didn't like the homesteading life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: She preferred being at home here in her own kitchen, in her own home where she could do things, and had things handy. And when you're on the homestead, you had to do just what you had, and nothing else. And she didn't like it. But she did. But I did. The youngsters all liked it, where they could put on overalls when everyone else was wearing dresses. They could put on overalls and run around and climb and do as they pleased. They liked it. Mama didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your mother what you would call a proper Victorian lady? A what? Was she a proper Victorian lady?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Because that was the Victorian days back then, they say. Well, not proper.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say she was proper Victorian, Bernadine?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Well, she wasn't smug, and she wasn't narrow. But she liked things convenient. She didn't particularly like the wood. You hurry up and come in here so I can hear you. She said she wasn't smug. Your mother wasn't smug. But she liked things convenient. She liked everything convenient. And that's one reason she didn't like homesteading. We always went out there, just as soon as school was out, or very soon after. We came in along in August, along about the first, between the first and the middle of August. And we were always so surprised when we got in to find that the haying was all done, and the harvesting was mostly finished the time we got in. And we hadn't seen it. Mother wasn't well. She was a regular. Oh, she wasn't well. And she landed her own bed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she sick much of the time?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I would say not well. She was on the mountain for a lot of time. But it wasn't until this time of year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hardship for?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes, it really wasn't for her. She was showing plenty. She couldn't go back. She'd rather have been home. But you know, it seems like such a difference in the generations, because if in your mother's generation, women didn't care for that kind of life, but the children liked it. The children liked it. But the mothers didn't. It was principally the convenience of things. And there was a little store out there that we could get things, but we usually packed most everything that we used from Moscow when we went out. That had a special pack made for us. And in that we could pack a good many boxes of different kinds of things. And this hack, we had to get a special bodied hack. And in order to protect us from the weather and all, we had the plumbers put posts up and across out of the piping. And then fastened the top on that. And the sides, if we wanted to put the sideburns down, we could put sideburns down. And that was a very convenient way to get out there. Mother usually had someone out with her during this summertime. Mrs. Lehman spent time out there with her. Mrs. McConnell spent some time. We had a hired girl that spent some time with us. And Mrs. Owens spent some time with us out there. So a great deal of the time we had additional company for Mother. And Mother and the company and the smallest youngster usually rode in a little single buggy. And the rest of us rode in the hack. One time we had a dog named Quigley. And there was a professor at the university named Quigley. And he had a head of very curly light hair. And this dog was a very curly dog. Although she was brown and white, we called her Quigley on account of the curls. And Dad decided in the summer that it was going to be too hot for the dog out there and for the trip. So he clipped her. And she was so ashamed after she was clipped, she crawled back under the big porch over there. And stayed and we could hardly beg her out or get her off to get her into the car. Or the convenience to take her out there. Well we got out to what was called Little Bear Creek. That's this side of Anderson out there. What used to be Anderson in those days. And she got to playing in the water in the stream. We all went back and under the bridge to eat our lunch in the shade. And she got to playing in the water and got soaking wet. And we picked her up and put her in the back of the rig in that wet condition. And after we got out to the cabin, the old dog began to wheeze and bark and wheeze and wheeze. And we didn't know what to do. Dad always had a medicine case that he gave Mother to take out for us for the youngsters if we needed anything. So Mother got out the medicine case and she looked up the fever tablets. And she forced these fever tablets down the dog. And we were out by the, pretty close to the first of the week. And Dad always came out the last of the week and hunted and fished in the stream. The little potlatch, at that time it had lots of fish in it. And he fished and he hunted for pheasants. And there was no limit on pheasants and no limit on fish. You could have what you wanted. And he's usually enough to take care of everything that he could get. Well, he was supposed to be out there the last of the week and here was a sick dog on his hands. So Mother dosed him and dosed him and dosed him. Until the time Dad got out, the dog was able to get up and wag his tail and greet him. So we took him hunting and fishing. But that was one reason that Mother didn't like the wood. Mother had a very fine mark when she could shoot any target. She was very good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: She'd go out before breakfast. She couldn't walk very far. She had no transportation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She said, what about?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: She'd go out and bring him pheasants again. She'd talk me down. And of course, they were in the movies. Dad got her little single barrel shotgun. He was so proud of it. And he was so proud of the way she could shoot. She could get birds anytime she took an ocean. That's all the fresh beans we had out there. Again, they're all in the movies.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did you usually pass the summer?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: The summer's out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do for entertainment? Were you a teenager when you first went out there?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would you do for your pleasure? For pleasure?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yeah, to enjoy yourself there. You could fish. You could hunt. You could walk the streams. And...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Huckleberry?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: You could bury. There were always berries there. Sometimes huckleberries later in the year. And... Once when we were out there, Mother, we ran out of jellies or syrups for pancakes. We ate lots of hotcakes. It was so easy to fix. It was so easy to take care of. And you could usually get some of the larger girls to make the cakes for the rest of them. So Mother had a chance there of resting while the rest of us worked. Then there was a little store about a mile or maybe a mile and a quarter called Collins. If you've been out in that private country, you know where the old store and the buildings were at Collins. And that was about a mile or maybe a mile and a quarter from our cabin. And we could go up there. There was usually lots of people around in the store. Homestaters coming in from their homes back in the Clarkfield country. And then the people at the town themselves. And it took some time to walk up and it took some time to walk back. So we kept you busy. Right behind our couch or cabin, we call it, was a stream. It was deep enough that we could walk up. Then you could swim in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, really?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: And the swim. Describe the swimming suit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Huh?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Describe the swimming suit. The swim. Suits. Oh, the swimming suit. Ha, the full dress. I'm ashamed to even think of them now. But they were suits. And the swimming suit. They had little under panties. Bloomers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hmm?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Bloomers. They fit down here with the rubber. Oh, that came down to your knees. And the little pant was fastened to a top vest. Or a... Sleeves. Well, it'd be just like a sweater without sleeves nowadays. But it had sleeves down to here on it. And practically low neck for that day. And the back came up across your back. And overall, from the waist down over these small trousers that hooked in at the bottom was a little skirt. Maybe the same material. Mine was a old gold skirt and a black trousers and a black top with a little old gold ring around the top. That is a suit. She didn't swim much. And now when you think of these bakinis and nothings that they wear... You can't support them. Oh no, nothing. You just wonder what the youngs just think. Well, they haven't known anything else. It's really indispensable. It sounds as though you couldn't swim very well with all that clothing on. There were stockings.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Stockings?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh yes, you had to have some stockings on. Black stockings. That sounds a little excessive to me. Black stockings and that's the trousers with the elastic at the top and that kept your stockings up. I can just see that. You had the black stockings and you had swimming shoes of some sort. You could either get old shoes and wear them or old slippers and wear them. Or you could wear these canvas regular swims. You know, that's what I mean when I say Victorian because it seems like such a different age as far as what was considered proper. It was a different age. You may call it Victorian. When I think of Victorian, I think back farther than that. It's farther back than Victorian. Mother was not a Victorian in my sense of the word because Victorian was still farther back. Your mother was born about 1858.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that get right into the Victorian era?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: The end. I think the turn of the century, the way I look at it, but that's from now, looking back at it from here, the turn of the century seems like the last part of the Victorian age. There was quite a change between the older people at that time and my mother's generation and our generation and quite a bit between mine and yours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think of the change as being between her generation and yours? Between the mothers and yours? How do you think of that change?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Well, I think it was for the better.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I mean what was the change? What was the change?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Well... Loveless, long skirts, high button shoes.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: All shoes, as Bernadette says, were high buttoned or high lace shoes came on after that. The high button shoes came on first, then the high lace shoes and your shoes usually came pretty well up on the leg. My hunting boots came up to my knees and my fishing boots. I used the hunting boots for both fishing and hunting, unless I was going with my father and I had a rubber, complete overall, a rubber suit. And he had one. And we used to fish the streams together. He'd work one side of it and I'd work the other and we'd fish the streams together. Sometimes we'd get in where the water's so deep that we'd have to support each other in order to get... That was a time when the Elk River was much larger and deeper than it is now. I just think that something... Mother's Better Dresses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or Mutton Leg Sleeves, do you know what that is? What?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Mutton Leg Sleeves.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mutton Legs?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: They were real big up here in Green Arrow down here. Pumped. And bustle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Bustles? What are they?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh, you may reveal some old stuff. They were... extended your ear. Oh yes, yes. Yes, I know. Now you know what to mean. I just didn't know what the word for it was. That's a bustle. This clothes are... the nicest clothes had bustles and mutton leg sleeves. And they were big puffs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What book were you looking at the other day that had the pictures of those old mutton leg sleeves?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I don't think it was a book. I don't think it was. I think it was the Time Magazine. They never had Time Magazine. You didn't play cards, you didn't read them. You'd have to read them out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would she dress up out on the homestead at all? No?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Well, she was already well dressed, but not with the mutton legs and the bustle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you say they'd play cards and read?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: They read, but not played cards. Well, you know, I'm interested too in the difference in values between her generation and yours. For instance, it seems like your independence in going out and taking a homestead yourself was a little bit different than the attitude of your parents' generation. It amazed everyone. Mother was raised in the country in a little place called Swan, Indiana. I think it had possibly a store and a school and maybe two or three houses. It was just a little joint, small country stopover. She was raised, her father and her brother had farms about a mile or a mile and a half from the town. She was raised and she walked from the home to the school each day. Mother had one large hip. We talk about bustles. She had this one large hip and she had to have a pad made on the other in order to hold the dresses out in a shape they should. And the pad was necessitated by this one large hip. And she claims that the large hip was for two reasons. One was from carrying the milk buckets and cream buckets from the barns to the house, which was quite a long distance, especially if they were carrying something. And the other was carrying her school books. She always carried them on the one, over the one hip. And she claimed that that was the reason her one hip was so much larger than the other. That was her lead for that. But she was not Victorian, but she was that in between. She still wore dresses that came to her shoe tops. You'd be surprised if you ever saw Mother in anything but a dress that came to her shoe tops. And she always wore rather dark colored gingham dresses. Once we got her a pink dress. And it was just beautiful on her. Her hair was turning gray and this pink and the gray hair was just beautiful. She liked it herself after she got it on and wore it. And the neighbor, Mrs. Shue, crossed the driveway here on the hill, over here where the little house is on now. She saw Mother's pink dress and she said she liked it so well that she got her a pink dress. I remember one day I said to Mother, this was green tapestry, not dark green, it was a lovely shade of green. It was little pink squares and lines. That's when we were dressing. Mother had beautiful hair.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well when she grew up, were her parents farmers?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So she probably had to work on the farm some herself to help out a little?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No, she didn't have the help. She was never strong enough for anything like that. And she carried the milk. That was her job, was to see that the milk was brought up to the house and put in the cellar. And Mother took care of the things in the cellar. That generation was quite against card playing. Now Father, when I was little, of course he had been used a lot when he was raised by a minister, but he wouldn't allow us to have playing cards. We could have all of his or his or something like that, but no playing cards. We didn't get the playing cards until we were about to college. And dancing with the blues. And when I was in high school and entering college, there was a very strict plan of when you enter the day. I would come around if I had a date on the part of her. Ten o 'clock she would raffle the door. Ten o 'clock, ten o 'clock, so my bow had to leave.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: That's how strict. Well, they were strict. You had a certain amount of discipline, which I don't think the youngsters have nowadays. Well, it wasn't that. You as a discipline, you just knew to please your parents and give up your date at ten o 'clock. You would go to movies, the guy would have an ice cream soda. That was a lot of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they religious, your parents?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No, especially. So this was more... less a matter of religious conviction than it was of what was right and proper. Actually it was right and proper. Father was always sure it was from the good ones. And we went to Sunday school. And I always argued with the teachers.

speaker: Bernadine Cornelison
words: Yeah, for looking the parts.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: It was the same with a great many of the older people. The Methodists used to use that old church that's down at the corner of 6th and Sepperson. That was the first Methodist church in Moscow. And the church people were very strict about those things. No dancing and no card playing. That was their idea of religion. In fact, we had a superintendent at the schools at that time by the name of Mr. Hedley. Used to live in this big house up on... let's see, it's Hayes and B, I believe. Possibly C. There was a large brown house up there that was the old Hedley home. And Mr. and Mrs. Hedley came to town and they were very well liked. And he was finally appointed as superintendent of the schools. And he... the congregation wanted music and a change of music. Then what the old superintendents liked and all. So someone got acquainted with Marie Cuddy. And... I've forgotten the girl's name. It was with her. It was Bess something. They were students at the university. Marie sang very nicely. And Marie and the friends played the violin. So they asked the girls to sing and play at the services that morning. Well the morning came. And the girls appeared ready for their part of the program. In the meantime Mr. Hedley had found out that there had been a dance at the university the night before. And both those girls had gone to the dance. And he would not allow them to play on the Sunday. So the girls went home. They never went back to church. But that... now that was his idea. And a great many of the others had the same idea of course. The part of the congregation were beginning to get the more modern feeling of the church. An ice cream social would be alright. But a violin in the church, they played violins at the saloons. And you're not going to have violin music in the church. That's all there is to it. So Mr. Hedley turned him loose. The very idea of attending a dance was wrong. Yes. To go to a dance. That was it. Besides violin music belonged in the saloons. And you could hear that every time. We had lots of saloons in Moscow at that time. And you would always hear the violin, the fiddle they called it, playing in the saloon. Therefore it could not be played in church. And the fact that the girls danced and had attended the dances was just that much too much. That's all there was to it. Saloons had swinging doors. They were all about the type of floor. I had the greatest desire to call them and see what went on. I never did. I was just dying. There were lots of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your mother opposed to, or was she in favor of the prohibition when it came in?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I suppose. I don't remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Prohibition?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Well, they never drinkers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Prohibition?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I wondered if your mother was in favor of it or not. If it mattered to her one way or the other. No, because she doesn't encounter it. Well, neither mother nor dad ever drank at that time. They did later. Dad had a couple of old hunting pals. And they would come up to the house on New Year's and would celebrate New Year's Day with a drink in the kitchen. Off the kitchen. And always, but they only stayed just a short time and they had their drink and they went home. But I was wondering. I thought of something I was wondering.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you tell me once about, you had made some, did you make some wine and hide it?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Now what was that, I don't remember that story. What I was going to tell you was the fact that dad, someone told us that you could make delicious wine out of dandelions. The gathering the blossoms in the spring and using it with raisins and orange juice and the dandelion, you heated the dandelion flowers. And then when the liquor did that, you put in the raisins and the orange juice and let it ferment and it made the delicious wine. Which it did. And dad was perfectly willing that we should gather the dandelions and take us out in the country along the edges of the fields to gather blossoms to see if we could make dandelions. And we could and we made the dandelion wine. At that time,

speaker: Ione Adair
words: that was during the prohibition days. And we had a county attorney here called Frank Moore. And he was a very tall, large, heavy set man and had flat feet. And he would always walk the alleys downtown to see if he could catch anyone that was drunk that he could see that they were taken care of. And he'd walk down, he had just one tune that he could whistle. And he'd whistle that tune from the time he left home, which is that high house on the bank on Jefferson Street. It's up high on the bank. And that was his home. The old Frank L. Moore home. And he'd leave there whistling. And he'd whistle all up and down these alleys and there were no flat feet that would go flop flop when old Frank would go. Dad had helped us get all these dandelion blossoms and Mother got the rest of the material. And we fixed it all together and we put it in the basement over there and let it ferment until it stopped fermenting. And then we bottled it. And Dad produced the bottles. He had bottles of what they called winola. Winola.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Huh?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Winola. Winola. We called it winola. Winola. And he got the bottles for Mother because he thought it had a tonic in it, which was good for Mother. And he brought up these bottles and they were about like, I guess they'd be gallon bottles. They'd be a bottle about like that.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: They'd hold about as much as a gallon. I think they were gallon bottles. At any rate, he brought these gallon bottles up and we bottled the wine. And we had it in the basement at the back part where there was no heat. And we thought we could keep it in there. On decoration day, Mother said to me, I'm afraid to have those bottles in the basement. If old Frank Elmore decides to investigate, he can make an awful big thing about Dr. Adair having bottles of wine in his basement during prohibition days. So she said, let's bury them. And out here, where this house stands now, we had an enormous great big bridal wreath bush. And we always kept it watered in the summertime. And Mother said, let's bury it out underneath that bush. So I went out and everybody else was out of town, out of the cemetery with their decoration and all. And I went out and dug a hole. A good, deep, large hole. And Mother and I carried those bottles from the basement in our aprons. We could carry two or three of the bottles at a time. We carried them up and we buried them under the bridal wreath bush. Where we thought that watering the bridal wreath bush would be significant. That no one would ever think there was anything buried there. So we dutifully buried the bottles and watered the bush all summer. Late that fall and just before freezing, Mother and I went out, dug up the bottles and put them back in the woodshed. And Frank L. Moore had lost his position as county attorney that time. Or city attorney, which was it. He was attorney anyway. He was attorney Frank L. Moore. And there was no danger. And we cashed our bottles back in the basement. I still have possibly a jigger. Half pint. About a half pint of that liquid in a bottle. It was a half pint bottle with the leather covering on the screw top. And the thing got tight and we never could get the top off. So the bottle is still full of Van der Laan wine. That's the only way prohibition affected my family. Father was always looking ahead. We never had wine in the house. But he said, well now with prohibition if we needed some wine we couldn't get it. So we started making wine. And the only time it was ever used, I think I used the first of it, we never drank it. But one time Mother said to me, you make the fruit cocktails. New Year's or Christmas. You make the fruit cocktails.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So I said, right?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: So I got a bottle of wine and poured a lot of this wine in on the fruit. And I did there Father said, I've never tasted such a good fruit cocktail. You always make the fruit cocktails with all of them. But with the wine. We're so proud. So it was Tenning Tonight, Tenning Tonight, Tenning on the Old Campground. It's the same song.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you speak about that Tenning Tonight, did you remember the revivals that they used to have here?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh we had numerous. They made me resign from the Methodist Church. Because they have a sawdust trail under big tents. And they get up and preach Hellfire and Brimstone. And they said if you dance or play cards you go straight to hell. I was about 13 or 14. And I knew that I was going to dance and play cards when I got older. So I just couldn't belong to the Methodist Church but I knew I was going to do that. So I went and took my name off the roll at the Methodist Church. And then I told my father what I had done. He didn't criticize me. He said I'm very disappointed. And he said, I wish you hadn't done it but it's alright it's your privilege. So I resigned. And that night over the next night, Papa had a dream. I went to heaven and said Peter wouldn't let me in. That's true. Because I was going to dance and play cards. We had Maori and Laurie and Moody. I think Billy was here once. I heard a guy named Bolgen came. Yes we had a Bolgen here. Maybe it was Bolgen. Laurie and Moody boys. We had a Bolgen. I cried on the show. I just couldn't take it. My father's sister's husband was a very profane man. He was a farmer who lived down at Johnson. And he was a very profane man. Only at the beach. And he decided once that he'd go down and hear Bolgen speak. He hadn't heard him. So he went down and he was really quite tame with the gentleman. And he continued going until the meetings closed. And Uncle John then decided that he was going to be a Christian. And stopped swearing and all. So he did try. And he eased up a great deal on his cussing. And he went down to the old home farm. After they had moved up here and lived over on Polk Street at that time. And they sold their place at Johnson. But he had this old friend, Mr. Thompson. Living in Johnson. And Uncle John decided to go down and see how Mr. Thompson was. And Mr. Thompson had attended a revival service down at the church in Johnson. And decided that he was going to become a Christian too. So the two men were conversing and having a very good time. Talking about the changes in their lives and so forth. And Mother called dinner. Mrs. Thompson asked the men to be seated and so forth. And Mr. Thompson felt just a little bit shy about saying grace. He'd been saying grace ever since he'd been converted. But he felt a little bit shy about saying grace. So he reached over and started passing the plates around for the dinner. And his little boy about, oh I guess he was four years, between four and five years old. Had been, well what do you... Accustomed. Accustomed to having the grace the last few days. And he was all ready for it. He had his hands folded and his head bowed. Papa didn't pay any attention. The youngster looked up and he says, Papa. And Papa went right on with what he was doing, serving the dinner. And the youngster looked up again and he says, Papa, pray. And Papa still didn't hear him. Finally he just swelled up and he put it up and he says, Papa, pray Daniel, pray. That's a true story, you see. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't that good?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: You know this revival is played so on people's emotions that at one time in Moscow, the majority of people stopped the Sunday paper. And the women would toss their jewelry into the baskets that they sent around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They stopped the Sunday paper?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes. The one that had the Sunday paper and people took out their jewelry and gave it to the revivalists. I remember that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the paper of the Moscow Sunday paper?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No, it was Spokane. They wouldn't take it on Sunday. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a real difference of opinion within the Methodist church about this? Are there some people that didn't want revivals?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: It certainly seems like... All of the churches in town contributed and had this big... Well, that's where the old Christian church stood there. At that time it was a post office corner. And they had this large tabernacle was built there for it. And all the churches in town contributed, both their congregations and their money. Jewelry and money. They're having this tabernacle built. They had real sawdust trails. They would walk down the sawdust trail and give their heart to God. Many times. And they'd cry on your shoulder. I didn't like it. Very emotional. Oh, very. It was very emotional.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say, Bernadine, they used to argue with the Sunday school teacher, over what?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Well, this one comes into my mind. I never learned anything about the Bible because she talked all the time. And she thought I was a sinner because I had withdrawn from the church. I still live at Sunday school. And she was talking about Martha and Mary. And Martha, Mary was the god that she did all these things for Jesus. But Martha did menial things. So she wasn't considered as good as Mary. And that made me cross. I said I think Martha was doing the menial things. And Mary got the credit for everything. And I just don't agree with that. Well, then we had an argument. That's the kind of discussion we had. She said, let me talk the Bible. I guess I'm always on the wrong side. I mean, I think differently. And the Sunday school teacher wouldn't accept my... The Bible says so in this belief, but don't doubt, have faith. And I wasn't quite ready for that. Well, it seems to me that what it is, you were thinking independently for yourself. I know. I always have. I don't know how I started sorority, but I did. A virtue, I think. Thank you. My father brought us up that way. It's like he said he didn't criticize me for withdrawing. He said he was disappointed. That hurt me in fact because I disappointed my father. But for me, I had to do it. But he didn't try to make you change your mind. No, no. Only because his dream was to be a child of his. But that's respecting you. Well, I certainly respected my father. I never... They were quite wise, I think. They were wiser than their generation. Because if I wanted to do something, Mother and Father would sit down, and say, well, now you tell us your side, and we'll tell you our side, and then you do what you want. They had me every time, but they never said so. I mean, I didn't want to go against their wishes. So they didn't have to say to you, you can't do it. They let you decide for yourself. I don't think I ever said can. I wanted to dance, but Father said when you get to college, you can dance. So I waited until I got to college. What did your parents think...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did your mother think when you decided to get a homestead of your own? To get a homestead of my own? What did they think?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh, they thought it was a thing to do. Everyone was taking up homesteads at that time. And lots of the people here in town had taken up homesteads. In that section of the country out there, there was an opening up of a large area of white pine timber out beyond Clarkia, about 45 miles beyond Clarkia.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: East?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: East, between Clarkia and Avery. I think there's a road now that goes from Clarkia clear across to Avery. But at that time, there was no road. It had to be trails, always trails. The Wallaces and the Watkins and the Martins and the Pulsons. Pulsons files. And the files. And the Wallaces, as I mentioned there. And the Torsons. Drugists and jurors and doctors. And Durhams. And all of these were Moscow people that had taken up claims in that section of the country. And Daddy knew the old homesteader, Matt Miles, who was locating people in that part of the country. And he knew Mr. Miles and he knew the family. The Calkins family and all. And so he thought if the... Winifred took up a claim out there. Winifred was the youngest daughter in the Calkins family. And Matt had married the older girl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Winifred's sister?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Winifred's sister. And then there was another. She worked in the post office.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Matt, had they taken up a claim too?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: They had homesteads out there. And were quite close to the land that Mr. Miles wanted to locate me on. And Mrs. Durham and Mrs. Tarson. And all. So we were just a Moscow community out in that section of the country. And they thought it was perfectly all right. They had no idea of the vigorous life they would have to live.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it your idea to do it? How old were you at the time? Were you still a teenager? Were you still a teenager at that time? Were you still in your teens at that time?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yeah. You had to be a certain age before you could take up a claim, I own. I think it was only 18. I'm not sure. Well, I was... I had finished high school and took the teacher's examination. And in order to pay for the claim, I taught two terms out of the Collins Schoolhouse. And I just had eight students. And I taught the school out there for two different terms.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it? You got $20 a month or something?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No, it wasn't... It was $38 to $40 a month. That was a big price for those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you stay when you taught?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I stayed at the Fry's who owned the store.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sam Fry?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No, Sam's brother, Abe. Sam, at that time, was not married. And his father and mother lived at Boville. You go out of Boville as if you were going to Elk River. And you take a left -hand turn and go up the creek. And Sam's father and mother lived up there. And Sam and Anna and Eddie and the rest of the family lived up there. There was quite a good -sized family and all. Then Sam later moved to town. And he and his wife bought what used to be the old Washburn heating house. And they turned it into... Then Mrs. Fry later, after Mr. Fry's death, turned it into a rooming house. And they used roomings in apartments only after that. Tell me what you had to do, what happened to you when you located on that homestead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When we were located?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: When you located, yes. Well, we were located. The man took us in and we had to go around to the different corners, the points of interest on the claim. We had to take in the corners of the claim, each one. And the man always took us, of course, what was said to be the corners. I suppose it was, I don't know. Not knowing that part of the country at that time. And May Calkins, his sister -in -law, had a cabin on the middle. And they were putting up a cabin for her. And my cabin was put within calling distance of her cabin. And when I stayed, I went out with Mrs. Durham and her daughter when they spent the winter out there. And we took our provisions in from Clarkia. And Herman Wilson that used to work were in the Wilson Seed Company down here, Washburn Wilson. And Herman Wilson was running the store at Clarkia at that time. We bought our provisions from them and they were packed on pack horses from there out to the cabin. And Mrs. Tarson had her house and her cabin just about a quarter of a mile from Mrs. Durham. And we had to cross one of these old boggy meadows every time we went out or in to town. Went across these boggy meadows and in order to get over to my house, my cabin and the May's cabin, and Mrs. Taylor's cabin, which was about a mile and a half beyond May's cabin. Mrs. Taylor was a sister of Griffin that run the dray here in town at that time. He was a big man. I think of a big man when my father moved to Moscow. All of his belongings for his office, his instruments and books and all, were in an enormous great big barrel. And they were shipped out by freight. And Griffin picked up that barrel and carried it up the stairway. It runs between Bjorklins and Creighton's. Carried up that stairway to Dad's office all by himself. That's the kind of a man he was. Well, his sister had the homestead next to May Cockins there. There was three of us in that ring. My cabin and May's cabin and Mrs. Taylor's cabin were all along there together. Not together really. Well, not together, but we called it together. All you had to do was to put on your shoes and walk one place to the other. You were quite some little distance, but you followed a trail across. That is, a trail on trees. It hadn't been used enough for there ever to be a foot trail there. You followed the trailblazers on the trees from one place to the other. But we got there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then what were the requirements that you had to meet?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Did you have to stay there for so many months of the... You had to have a clearing. You had to have a certain amount of land cleared and ready for cultivation. And you had to have a garden or a crop. And you had to have your cabin. And you were supposed to be there all the time. You were homesteading. You were supposed to live on your homestead. That's the reason my father had us go out every summer on the homestead next to Collins there. It was because he couldn't leave his office and we took the homestead duties and lived on the homestead. You were supposed to live on the homestead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many months? How many months were you supposed to live on?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Well, at least six months at a time. And when you're out there and so far away, you don't come to town. You don't have horses. Or you have to stand out if you get a chance to have a packer come in with horses for you. So, I had a horse of my own and May Cockins had a horse. And Mrs. Taylor walked. She was a tall, strong woman and she walked any place. It's funny to see her walking on snowshoes. She was tall and she brought one foot this way and one foot this way. And the snow would just fly up across the back of her clothes in the back. We had a man staying with us out there by the name of Carter. He looked down the trail one day and he said, Here comes the Georgie Oaks. The Georgie Oaks was a steam pedal steamer on the Cardellain Lake at that time. You don't hear about Georgie Oaks now, but Georgie Oaks was a pedal steamer. And worked on the Cardellain Lake. And he looked down the trail and saw Mrs. Taylor coming up on her snowshoes and snow just flying. And he said, Here comes the Georgie Oaks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't she the one that used to carry her bacon and her bosom?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No, that was Mrs. Paulson. I remember when she tried to do it. Yeah, she just picked up all her pound of bacon and her slant. Mrs. Paulson was a homesteader.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How can I locate her?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: On the road to Lewiston, over the snow hill, you go past a little wishing well, I call it. It's a well which has been enclosed and has the frames up over it. And it sets back away from the road on the left -hand side as you're going towards Lewiston. And that was the former Paulson homestead. The father took up a homestead out there. Later they moved into town and lived in this gray house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember where Charles Carso lives down below?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Well, it's just beyond that. In that gray house, a two -story gray house. And they moved in there. Mrs. Paulson took up a homestead out on what was the Floodwood Creek. And her daughter, Mabel, went with her. Her daughter was a very good singer. And she sang with the Metropolitan Opera for a while. She married Louis DuBoisne, a musician. She's still living. She is still living, I think, Mabel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is she still?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Well, she was over at the house a year or so ago. Well, she is an aunt of Mrs. Kinzer who lives on Pobus Weber. She and DuBoisne were divorced. And she married a man with the name of Weber that lived in Palouse. And Elmer Paulson was county commissioner, one of the county commissioners for years, of the courthouse here. And they lived here in town. And she homesteaded out there with you. She homesteaded out there, too, Mrs. Paulson. Yes, she homesteaded. And she's the one. And Mabel always went with her mother when she went out. Because Mrs. Paulson felt that she had to have company. But this time she didn't go with her. And I don't know just why, but mother was coming out from the homestead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Our mother?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No, Mrs. Paulson was coming out from the homestead. And it began to get rather late in the afternoon. And she decided she'd just stay there at Collins at night. And not try to come along or go as far as Boville. So she decided that if she stayed there, right next to the fence outside of the woman that run the little restaurant, that she would fry some bacon for herself. And she carried the bacon in her blouse, in the front of her blouse. So she had bacon in her pourer. She had her bacon and she went in and asked the lady of the house to drop an egg in the tea kettle for her. So she had hard boiled eggs and bacon that evening. And then instead of staying all night, she decided to come on down to our place. About a mile and a quarter farther. She'd make it down there. And she made it on down to our place. And we were all sleeping, all the young people were sleeping in an old log barn which was across the driveway from the cabin. And so we took Mrs. Paulson in to sleep in the barn. I think she was a sponger.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Huh?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I think she was a sponger. She was, no doubt about it. But she saved money and she saved the farm and she gave a lot of money to Mabel for her education and to Elmer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she stay with you for long? Did she stay for long when she got there?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No, she stayed all night at our place but she came on the next day. She was headed from Moscow and was taking it afoot. And that's the only way she had it traveling from Moscow. This place they call the barn has never been used as a barn. It's a log, a log building. And the mattresses, tell me about the mattresses. The mattresses are made of pine boughs or fur boughs. You know how to make a fur bough. And then over the top of that we put a layer of hay that we got down at Bogle off of the meadows. And we put that. But we had nice beds and we had plenty of room so we had a suite there. Well, Mrs. Paulson had heard some wild tales about the cougars down in that part of the country. She had been bothering the stock on the Bogle Meadows and on the Fry Meadows at Collins. And so she was very anxious to sleep in the barn with the rest of it. The boys up at Collins decided that they'd play a joke on Mrs. Coleman. She played a joke on the old woman running the restaurant.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Coleman or Paulson?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Ate her bacon and eggs outside after boiling the eggs in the tea kettle. And so they decided that they'd play a joke on Mrs. Paulson. So they got an oil can. I don't know whether you've ever used an oil can with a rosin string. You take a rosin string and put it in an oil can. Take the top out of the oil can and fasten the rosin string in one end of the oil can. And pull on this rosin string and it gives a hideous shrieking noise. Well they fixed the oil can and the troop up there, there was five or six of these boys all together. And they came down and in the woods just across the creek from our barn, pulled the strings on this oil can and made this hideous noise. Well Mrs. Paulson was wide awake in a moment because she'd heard these tales of the cougars. And she was sure there was a cougar. So she was up and getting ready to go into the house with Mother and Mrs. McConnell. Well we told her we didn't think that the cougars would ever disturb us. And she said, well won't you close the door, fasten the door then so that they can't come in. And she said they might come in that window. There was a window. Well about that time the boys would pull that string again and here would be this awful noise. And they carried that on for quite a while until Mrs. Paulson was just beside herself. And then it stopped. And they went on back up to town and the next morning, were they back bright and early to see whether the noise had disturbed anybody down at our house. Mrs. Paulson got up quite early and after having a little breakfast with Mother, she started on down towards Moscow. She evidently made it because she got home all night. But she was so funny.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you girls know what it was? Making the noise?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: We knew what it was. So it didn't bother us. We didn't tell her we knew what it was. Oh no. That was part of the joke. That was part of the game. Do you know there used to be a little string that came down that side of my memory of Volvo. It was called Ruby Creek. We went down and dug Ruby. That was one of my places.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it what?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Ruby Creek on my Volvo. Oh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What makes you say that she was a sponger?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Because she went outside the cafe, she filled a little fire and pulled out her bacon and fried it. And went in the cafe and asked them to boil her eggs in their tea kettle. That's right. I think that was the poor taste. Uh huh. Worse than that. Instead of going in and paying for a meal, she fried her bacon outside along the side of the fence. And that was my childish reaction but it still happened. But she didn't want to eat soft boiled eggs so she asked the lady at the house to boil her eggs in the tea kettle for her. So I don't know whether she boiled them in the tea kettle or not. That's something she talks not to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know, were you close to Round Top Mountain? What? Were you close to Round Top?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes. Because you know the name of the creek there that runs south of Round Top Mountain is Adair Creek.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: There's a creek that runs south of Round Top Mountain that's called Adair Creek. Uh huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the mom who climbed and left her name in the spank of can?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No, that was at Elk Butte that we left her. They must have named that creek after you. They did. Well, that was named after me too. That came after 1910. After 1910, the fires. Yes. And we had a neighbor, a Mr. Flower. And Mr. Flower had an uncle from Iowa that had been spending the summer with him. And the smoke was getting pretty bad up in that part of the country then from those Wallace fires and all the other fires.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: And Mr. Flower decided that he would bring Mr. Well, I can't think of the uncle's name right now. But he would bring the uncle down to Avery and put him on the train and send him back to Iowa. And in the meantime, the firefighters had left Mrs. Taylor's Creek and that branch of the potlatch where they were fighting the fire in 1910. And then it was time to move on to another location. And while they were moving, they let us, Mrs. Taylor and I were cooking for these firefighters. And they let us go down to Mrs. Durham's place. And Mrs. Durham and her daughter were living there. So we decided when we got there that we wouldn't wait for the firemen. We'd wait and let them tell us. They said it would take about so many days before they could get set up for us. And we would go over to Mr. Flower's. So we walked that four miles over to Mr. Flower's place and stayed all night. And Mr. Flower's told us he was coming down to Avery to bring the uncle out. And we could go with him if we wish. So Myrtle and I decided to go with Mr. Flower's to take the uncle out. And Mrs. Durham stayed with Mrs. Flower's and the little girl. They had a small child. So we started off and we got down. When we got on the tops of those long barrier ridges going down from, not Round Top, but Debitts Basin. There's a long ridge that comes down. We could see the fires off on both sides of us. On both sides. When we got down into Avery, we were met before we crossed the bridge at Avery by an officer. He told me this part. He told us to turn around and go back. And we told him that we couldn't do that. That we had to put this old gentleman on. So we were on the train there for two days. And it was during that time that they told us that they were going to take us up the line for a little ways to see how things were going. We could go up with them. We went up with them and they told us then that they were going to call that creek up there. A dare creek. It had to have a name. It didn't have any name so they called it a dare creek.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was after you had cooked for these firefighters wasn't it?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: That was after you had cooked for these firefighters. Oh yes, we cooked for the firefighters up there. We came down to Avery so that Mr. Flowers could start his uncle. That's the way you got out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you live right near a dare creek yourself or were you fairly far from there?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: It was 28 miles from Avery back to our homestead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the creek that you were nearest to? Nearest?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Just a small stream. I don't think it had any name at that time. Just a small stream that run through a meadow. There was a stream by Mrs. Durham's place. We called that just the river I guess. It wasn't much of a stream. It couldn't fish in it. It wasn't large enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you get your water when you were on the homestead?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: On a spring. We dug a spring.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is who?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: You had to have water and we dug a spring.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much of a community life there among you homesteaders? Was there much of a community life there among you homesteaders?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No, they all were so far apart. See, Mr. Flowers was about four miles and Hanson and Larson were about the same distance only in another direction. And Mrs. Pyle was over on Floodwood Creek and Mrs. Taylor and Miss Calkins and my place and Mrs. Tarison and Mrs. Durham. And we were all, well it was about four miles from my place back to Mrs. Durham's place. And then Mrs. Taylor was still farther on. So there was not very much between us. At Easter time, Bill Griffin, nephew of Mrs. Taylor's, spent the winter out there with her and she wanted to do something special for Easter. So she sent out, when Bill went out to get the mail, and she sent out for a chicken. And a real civilized chicken. And she cooked this chicken and we had chicken for Easter. An Easter Sunday chicken. Bill and Mrs. Taylor and Myrtle and myself, the four of us. And we got away with the chicken too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a birthday party once on the roof of your cabin? In who's?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: On the roof of your cabin you had a party or something. Oh, Myrtle had a birthday. Her birthday was in April, right along the first of April. It's either the first or the second of April. And Hanson and Larson, who had this homestead four miles over, just two boys, Larson was staying with Hanson and Hanson owned the homestead. And they came over, they knew it was about time for Myrtle's birthday, so they came over to our cabin and said they'd come for the birthday. They got the only place we could think of that would be high and dry, there was still snow all over everything out there in April, was the roof of the cabin. And so we took blankets and put up on the roof of the cabin and crawled up on the ladder and sat on the cabin while we had our birthday dinner. Well, after dinner Myrtle decided that she'd make candy for the boys. So she went down to make some fudge for the boys. And she made part of the fudge with lots of pepper in it. So that when the boys bit into this candy they got their mouths full of pepper. So she had two batches, one of the pepper kind and one without pepper. And she and I were eating off of the one dish and the boys were eating off of the other. Well, they finally got so they couldn't stand it any longer, so they had to go down and find some water. And they found the water and rinsed their mouths out and tried to get the burning stopped in their stomachs. And about that time it was time for them to start home. And they did. I think they made it by night, although they had a four mile trip across country. And across the stream and all. Before they left, they both wrote out a will on a cigarette paper. They always rolled their own because you couldn't carry enough cigarettes to keep them going out there. But they could take in the tobacco and the papers and roll their own, so they rolled their own. And they rolled their own cigarettes and on the cigarette paper each one wrote out a will. And if they didn't make it home that night, that we were to have such and such a thing that were at the cabin. And they left the cigarette papers with us. And they went on home. Well, they were funny things. Always something coming up. They were.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: So I don't know so many things that I couldn't even begin to tell you. What's funny is that it seems like you really invented things and became resourceful when you didn't have things around you to give you. You just had to do things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I told you I think about Carter, the man that stayed with us that run the trapline, didn't I?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: He told us about Silvestre. He told me about him. About the man. And about Silvestre. And Silvestre.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Carter stay all winter? Did he stay all winter?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes, he stayed all winter with us. He stayed until late spring. Then he left us after the snow land so that he could get off and go to work in the out country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it just you that he helped or were there other homesteaders around that he helped out too?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No, he only helped just us. My father and Mrs. Durham made the arrangements for him to stay with us at so much a month. And he was to stay there and to help us with the wood and with the heating and the snow and anything that needed. And they had to go out for the mail. He went out once a month for the mail.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To Clarky?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Carter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he go to Clarky? To Clarkia? For the mail?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes. No, Avery. Avery. 28 miles to Avery. 45 miles to... 28 miles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what about the hardships of that, Ione? Were there things that you had to do that were hard, that were difficult?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh, only in sawing or getting your wood or anything like that if you happened to be low on it. Nothing especially hard. See, now you would consider it a hardship, but then it was just a part of the life. The man that stayed with us took care of that. That's why my father insisted on having someone stay with us so that if anything happened, if we get cut or anything like that, that we would be able to get word out for help or to get ourselves for help. She went to the most vigorous in years. And she's the healthiest of the family. Kind of interesting. Uh huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you, during the summer though, you didn't need anyone to stay with you then?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No, we didn't need anyone especially. There were three of us at the one place and we could go back to Mrs. Taylor's the other way. But hers was quite a ways because it was four miles from Mrs. Durham's over to my place. And Mrs. Taylor was a mile and a half beyond that. And her nephew had left before the fires and before the fire season started. So when these firefighters came through there looking, trying to locate some way of getting into this fire section, they wanted to know if the trail went any farther. And we told them it went about a mile and a half to Mrs. Taylor's. And so they went on and wanted to know if they could go on. We said that they could, that they could go on over. Well when we got over, we found that they had found the cabin and they wanted to know when we arrived if we wouldn't get breakfast for the men. So we got breakfast for those five or six men that were working, looking for trails. And when the rest of them caught up, they wanted to know if we wouldn't continue. So we stayed on then as cooks for the firefighters. A place to go except for the fire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go back after the fire? Did you go back to the homestead after the fires were over?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes, after the fires were over we went back to the homestead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your place burned?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No, the fire didn't come that way. The wind was the other way. And the fires from Wallace came up but they followed the ridges up and didn't get into our part of the country at all. They were in on the flood wood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many years did you stay on the homestead?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: We were in there from, let's see it was, we went in to stay definitely. We were in part of the time during the summer before in 1909. And then we were there all the summer 1910. And I went back then afterwards in the early 1911 to see how things were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the time I went with you?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: You were with me in 1909. Marjorie was. Marjorie was with me in 1909. I wasn't dead here in the fire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of it, Bernadine, of the homestead when you were there?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: It was a long ride. I'd never ridden horseback before. We rode in from Carcass, 45 miles. All in one day I believe, we rode 24 hours a night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I keep saying, how much farther is it?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh, about an hour. Well, an hour and a half, about four hours. That meant nothing to me. I thought we were never going to get there. We had to cross this body by the way. One horse had gone down. They never could get it out. The horse would leave from one touch to the grass. I wasn't too happy on the ride. I loved it after I got there. My own was so entertaining. She would take me out and teach me different flowers and different kinds of pine. And we told her about the bird that I treated. And we learned poetry. She had a book of Robert's service. I think I told you about that. We actually decided together. We learned it together. She takes one line and I take the next line. We can do it now I think, but we have to be together because I don't know the line in between. But she's very inventive. She's a clever person.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you then?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I was about 12, 13. It was quite an adventure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of the cabin itself? Did it seem ample to you?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it seem rude and just real primitive?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Very primitive. Just a one room apartment. But when I was a little girl from about 2 to 7, we went to a Collins cabin. So I was kind of accustomed to what a cabin would be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what about Friar Owen living there by herself so long? Doesn't it seem quite remarkable?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I can't imagine my father in the hallway. I just can't. But for an issue I'm doing so it's alright. He worried about her. God I know, especially with our season, many people were killed by Friar. We didn't have any word from Ione for about 5 or 6 weeks. Till this time I heard a son of a mother cry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just out of worry about Ione?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: All these people had been burned. They went into a cave and smoked and gassed us. It was a sick season. And that time we had no word at all. And I remember mother broke down and cried at the same time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When she heard the news of these other people dying?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Not knowing where Ione was. We didn't know she was with the firefighters you see. And every indication was that that whole area had been burned over. So then daddy and daddy go on a trip to Alaska with two other girls. Which was primitive at that time. And the one thing they came back on, went down and all these people were drowned. And that was the word we got. So again, called them out about why they lost Ione. But they stayed over and they walked a sick, got a big of us. And took another boat. So she missed it at that time. She's lucky. She's talking very loudly. Sometimes she gets quite confused. And very very tired. Before she was in the hospital she was... But wouldn't have done you any good. Probably. But after the oxygen treatment and the antibiotics and the back to, I mean, normal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you like a cup of tea? Would you like a cup of tea?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: We've been talking a lot. No, I don't think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No, I guess not. I'll be glad to serve. I don't think very good tea. I'll serve a teacup. That's fine. A little peaco. Put it aside, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Orange peaco?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yeah. And little bags. I'm not very domestic.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you talking about?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh, we were talking about the homestead. Mostly. I was impressed by your homestead. I said I was, in my younger years, had been brought up going to the summer down here. So I knew what to expect when you said cabin though. I'll tell you, my wife was a lookout.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She was?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes. And she was the first woman lookout that they had on Union District in Willougha -Whitman, which is near Baker, Oregon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oregon?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: And she was the first one they had in that district. And all these men in that district were sure that she was going to quit after the first fire. That she'd be down. Because they didn't think a girl would want to be. It was on the homestead with me and we couldn't think of anything to do. We told him that thing. We told him about the services pawns.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I'd forgotten that we told him that. She, we were just talking about it. But we had to be together to be excited.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You really found it to be an adventure?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes. We enjoyed it. We enjoyed it. We had had times together there at Cowan. And I remember as a child we used to go out from Bowville. There were always plenty of bows around you, my field of sisters. And Carrie Bush, but the boys would come down from Bowville at night. They'd sit around the campfire and sing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have marshmallows then to toast after Halloween? Have what? Did you toast marshmallows around the campfire?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I don't get it, Bernadine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you toast marshmallows around the campfire?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh. You would all sit around and sing. Nobody knew anything about marshmallows then. There was lots of singing. And lots of bows.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I didn't know anything about marshmallows until after we got into town after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But then, what did the, how did it work out then?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: The government didn't let you prove up on the line.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't let me prove up? How did that happen?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: They said they hadn't been out there long enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what would it take us?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: And that we hadn't homesteaded, we didn't take it up as homestead. That we just took up for adventure and so forth. And they, um, Mr. Rhodes, Ashley J. Roach, who was sent out from Washington to inspect all the homesteaders in that section of the country. And he said that nobody could live out there under those conditions. That it just weren't living conditions. You couldn't do it. As we spent 22 months at a time out there, straight through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had you lived there the full time? You lived there 22 months running?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: 22 months. I was only out once during that time. And that was to the hearing in Wallace. The homesteaders were all called in to Wallace to give their version of the story. But Ashley J. Roach had his version and he'd been sent there by the government. And therefore the government took the government's side to it. And Mrs. Tarson lost hers. This is, uh, how about Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Durham?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I'm not sure about Mrs. Durham because they moved in to Spokane afterwards. And I'm not sure whether they did or not. And Myrtle, the daughter, married a man in Spokane, a Mr. Melcher. It wasn't a gas station, I think it was a repair station and gas station in Spokane. And they finally, he had to give up the work and she had rheumatism and arthritis so badly that they had to go into a nursing home out from Spokane. And my niece went out to visit with them a couple of times and to see them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about you? Did they, they didn't allow your homestead either?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No, they didn't allow mine. I have a letter from Bertonell French that said he'd gone to certain committees and told them. And knew that people could live under those conditions. Because lots of people were doing it and all, but it had been, the decree had been handled down and I lost my homestead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell them about the last episode about that? The what?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: The last episode about the man that met you out from our field.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Oxford station?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yeah. This same Ashley J. Roach that was at the head of the inspection for the homesteaders out there. And he had been appointed at the head of the Oxford ranger station out from, from Orphino. Up past Weyife and Pierce. And Pierce and out. And Bernadine and I were spending the summer with Mrs. Arnett or Mrs. McBride that used to be here in town. And we were spending the summer with her. And Mr. Arnett wanted to take us down to the Oragrand, to the river on a fishing trip. And so he packed the horse and we got all ready and went over to the Oxford ranger station.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And at the Oxford ranger station they were having some sort of a, what do I call it?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: A party of some kind for the people at the station and homesteaders. But Mrs. Arnett didn't know who was giving it. But she said it was and we'd stop there on our way down to the Oragrand and see what was going on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And so we got to the Oragrand to the ranger station and whom should I meet?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: But Ashley J. Roach who had been sent out from Washington in the first place. And he said, it's been some time since I've seen you and I said it surely has. And I don't think any more of you than I did then. He said, I'm sorry for that. He said, had I been used to the West or been acquainted with the West in any way, I would never have sent in the reports that I sent in to the government. I said yes, but you caused me to lose my homestead. And numerous others in the same. He said, I know, but it was all my fault because I sent in the reports and the people there were supposed to take the opinion of the man they sent out to inspect. He just didn't know. He didn't understand the conditions. He just didn't understand. He didn't know a thing about the West. He didn't know a thing about homesteading. He still felt a little guilty I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I think he still felt a little guilty. I think he still felt a little guilty. Oh. But he remembered. He was there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you forgive him?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: She said, no. I forgive him, but I didn't forget him. I still have the same resentment as far as my homestead is concerned. But against the man personally, I can see his side of it. See, that's the way we were brought up. Give your opinion, I can laugh because I didn't leave it to choice. We had quite a talk that day. We just sat over in a corner by ourselves and let the musicians and the dancers have their fun. Ashley J. Roach and I sat over and hashed over old times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did pretty much everyone lose their homestead in that area that you were homesteading?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes, there were a lot of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They took it away from everyone? Or from most of the people? They took the homesteads away from most everyone in that area?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes, nearly everyone. Mrs. uh... Mrs. Tarson. I think Mrs. Durham and Mrs. Tarson got theirs. Mr. Flowers lost his and the boys lost theirs. And I think Mrs. Fyle lost hers. I wonder why that discrimination, why some would and some wouldn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know that? Why did some lose them and others not lose them?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I don't know. I don't know what there was about it. His inspection and his means of reporting it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have some land cleared? Did you have some land cleared on the homestead? Did I have what? Some land cleared? Land? Cleared?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Cleared. Did you... Oh, yes I had some cleared. And then the meadows we were using for hay for the horses. And there was lots of the timber. We had to have the timber cleared from around the cabins. And all that we could, we had lots of brush done. And for the time we were there we had a very good showing. Of living up to it, the law, and doing as we were supposed to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You lost a lot of money on that deal, didn't you?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: They had to hire a man. A lot of time, too. Well, I think she enjoyed it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, if people, if those people had been able to prove up, spend the five years and prove up on the homesteads, do you think most of them would have eventually sold their land to the timber companies?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes, they would have sold to the lumber companies. That's what they took it for. Lots of them, those that did have it. Those that were allowed to take their claims, to keep their claims.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I know there was a differentiation between taking a homestead and having a claim with mineral rice, wasn't there?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Maybe that was the difference. I don't know. I kind of remember something like that. There was railroad land right close, just across the creek and up a very short ways from my property. And that was probably, and for May Cockin's property. Maybe the Union Pacific property. And it could have been that the lobbyists for the railroads were responsible for that. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much were the timber companies paying for the timber on 160 acres of good timber? Were they paying a lot of money?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I couldn't tell. You were approximated. What daddy got for his.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he get?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I think it was about 3500. 3500 dollars. About 3500 dollars. That was at Bowville. Just at Bowville there. And that of course was considered the old homesteaders. And those people had been in there for years.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Daddy sold that claim in order to have the cash to make the down payment on this house. So I remember that was the first time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much did this house cost him? Total?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: About 5000 I think. So that was most of the money that he needed. We sold it for 5000. The property cost him just about what he got for the timber claim.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: But that was this whole block.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The whole block?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: The church was up there. I think that was the only part of it. Not including this property. Well it seemed to me then that I think that's part of what the government was arguing. That if people wanted to, were planning to sell to the timber company then they shouldn't be allowed to homestead it. Which seems wrong to me. That I think that's part of the way they were. There's something about mineral rights that went into that too. I don't remember enough about it. I remember Daddy talking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember Frank Robinson very well?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: That fellow that Mr. Robinson... I didn't know him. I read one of his books. The Tale on the End of a Pig or something like that. Blood on the Tail of a... Robinson!

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What Robinson?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Frank B. Robinson. The Psycheanna fellow. I have just one recollection of Mr. Robinson. I went down to the... What was the... Owl... Not the owl, but... Bull's Drug Store. The drug store right at the corner of Main and... and the first... Third Street. And I had an umbrella with me. It had been raining when I left home. I had an umbrella with me. And I stepped into the drug store to get something. I don't know what it was. And they had put out little shelves like this. The counter was up here and then this little shelf. And they had put lots of little odds and ends of things in these different... shelves for people... so people could see what they had. And I stepped up to the counter and asked for what I wanted. And this stranger waited on me. And I thanked him. And turned around and went outside the door. When I got outside, I raised my umbrella again. And a little package. A line of candles. A little box of candles. About that large. You know those little tiny candles that's put on birthday cake. Dropped out of my purse. Out of my umbrella. Well I turned right around and went back into the store. And handed the package to the man as he stepped out again. And said when I raised my umbrella this fell out. So it must have caught on the umbrella when I was standing here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, oh yes?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I can imagine that that's the true story. And I turned on my heels and walked out. I didn't go in the store for a long time. I told my father about it when I came home. And he said, that's the new man. That's Psyche Anna Robinson. He's the man that's going to build here. He's going to work at Bowles Drug Store. And he said there are other drug stores in town. And he said I'm known in all of them. So I didn't go in for a long long time into the drug store. I have a very poor recollection of Frank Robinson. And also his religion.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I never did like him.

speaker: Bernadine Cornelison
words: Eventually.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: We're speaking about the change in the times. We had always ridden horseback. My father had horses that we could ride. And we had always ridden horseback. And then decided that horseback with the side saddle was not an easy way for a person to ride. And that we should have stride saddles. And in order to have stride saddles, we had to have divided skirts. Well, we got the ladies to make us some divided skirts. And we wore divided skirts and we rode stride saddle. This was unusual for the time. This was unusual for the time. Oh, the town. Why everybody thought it was awful for us to be riding side saddle. Stride saddle. Everybody else wore ladies saddle. With the horn on them and the hook on it. And for girls to be riding stride was something most unusual. But my sister and I both rode stride saddles. And we had divided skirts that went with it. And you didn't care that it was unconventional. It didn't bother you girls at all. Dad said it was alright and it was the thing to do and that's what we did. Safer. We rode horseback all of our lives after that. We rode in the timber and we rode on the trails and we rode everywhere.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How rough was it to get around in the high water of spring out there? Out there?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: When the snow went off, it went off very gradual. The streams across these meadows. And the meadows got boggier than they were before. But we could still manage to get across them by being very careful where we walked and all. The horses, when they would come to those meadows, always stopped. Shook. They just did not want to go in on that boggy land. And the locators, when we first went out, the locators had a hard time getting the pack horses across the meadows. But the meadows dried up then. The horses got used to being able to pick their way around. And they picked their way around in the meadows more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mentioned that you used to work on quilt pieces out there? What?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Quilt pieces.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do that much?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh, I worked on quilt pieces because at the time I told you that we were taken from Avery down to Tico on coming home. You said that Mann there remembered your quilt pieces. Yes, the conductor asked me what we did out there. And I said we piece quilts and we do this and we do that.
